segments.s557-g33: Literal order is subject–negation–predicate: ستیزه (a noun 'strife/quarrel') is the theme, نه + آید negates the verb phrase 'good comes'. از نامجوی is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the name-seeker' modifying آید. The clause therefore says that 'good does not come from a name‑seeker.' Note that ستیزه is used as the thing affected by the statement ('strife' or 'quarrels'), not as an adjective.
segments.s557-g34: بپرهیز is a 2nd‑person imperative 'avoid / refrain (from)'. و simply links the two imperatives. گرد here functions adverbially as 'about/around' (go about), so گرد ستیزه = 'to go about (seeking) strife'. مپوی is the negative present/imperative form of پوییدن 'to go/seek' (the negative prefix م-), giving 'do not go/seek'. Together: 'Avoid (him), and do not go about seeking strife.'
segments.s557-g35: This is a triadic address listing types: سپاهی (soldier), دهقان (peasant), و بیکار (the idle). The following word شاه is vocative 'O king' addressing the ruler rather than modifying بیکار. So the speaker is saying to the king: consider these three categories.
segments.s557-g36: چنان دان is an imperative phrase 'know/consider thus' (چنان = 'thus/so', دان = 'know/consider'). که introduces the subordinate content clause. هر سه ندارند راه is a present indicative: ہر سه 'all three', ندارند 'they do not have' (3rd‑pl. negative), راه 'a way/path' as the direct object placed after the verb; literally 'all three have no way.'
segments.s557-g37: به خواب 'in sleep' is a locative phrase; اندرست is an archaic/stylized verb meaning 'is within' or 'lies in.' آنک is a relative pronoun 'the one who/that which', followed by بیکار بود 'was idle' (بود as a past/state copula). The clause expresses a habitual/general truth: 'He who is idle lies in sleep.'
segments.s557-g38: پشیمان شود uses شود (3rd‑singular) 'becomes' to mean 'he becomes remorseful.' پس 'then/afterwards' is adverbial. چو introduces a temporal clause 'when', and بیدار بود uses بود (here perfective/past or general-state copula) with بیدار 'awake' — 'when he is/was awake.' Together: 'Afterwards he becomes remorseful when he wakes.'
segments.s557-g39: ز is the poetic/prepositional form 'from/of' introducing the source. گفتار نیکو 'good speech' and کردار زشت 'bad/ugly deed' are coordinated by و, presenting a moral contrast (good words paired with bad actions). The phrase sets up what follows as the consequence of that mismatch.
segments.s557-g40: ستایش نیابی is a negated predicate: ستایش 'praise' is the object and نیابی (negative form of یابیدن) means 'you will not attain/find' (general statement). نه introduces a second negative coordinated item: خرم بهشت 'the joy/bliss of paradise' — thus a correlative negation: 'neither praise nor the bliss of paradise will you obtain.' نیابی here functions as a general/future‑oriented negated assertion.
segments.s557-g25: تن شاه is a possessive noun phrase (literally 'the body of the king'); دین را uses را to mark دین as the definite/beneficiary NP ('for the faith'); پناهی بود is a past-copular clause 'was a refuge'. Together: 'The king's body was a refuge for religion.'
segments.s557-g26: که introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for/that'); دین is the subject of the clause; بر سر او is a locative prepositional phrase 'on his head' (بر = on, سر = head, او = him); کلاهی بود is a nominal predicate 'was a hat' (بود = was). The clause gives the reason/explanation for the previous line.
segments.s557-g27: خنک stands as a predicate/adjectival praise ('fortunate/pleasant/calm') placed before the relative clause; آنک functions as a relative pronoun 'who/that'; در خشم is a locative phrase 'in/under anger'; هشیارتر is the comparative adjective 'more alert/clear‑headed' (تر = comparative suffix). Word order is typical poetic: predicate + relative clause describing the praised person.
segments.s557-g28: همان = 'that same / the very one' (picks up the person just described); بر زمین is a locative phrase 'upon the earth' (بر = on, زمین = earth/ground); او refers back to that person ('he'); بی‌آزارتر is comparative 'more harmless' (i.e. less harmful). This clause states the practical consequence: the same prudent man does less harm in the world.
segments.s557-g29: گه is an adverb 'sometimes'; دست تنگی is a genitive/compound meaning 'narrowness of hand' = poverty or lack of means; دلی (here) denotes 'a heart' and introduces the quality that follows; شاد و راد are two coordinated adjectives 'glad and generous' describing that heart. The clause says poverty need not prevent a joyful, noble heart.
segments.s557-g30: جهان is the subject 'the world'; بی‌تن here is used poetically as 'bereft/devoid' (literally 'without body') modifying جهان; مرد دانا is 'a wise man/person'; مباد is the negative optative 'may it not be' — together: 'May the world not be without a wise man.' The optative (مباد) at the end negates the existence of such a state.
segments.s557-g31: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/if'; بر دشمنی ... بر توانا uses بر twice to mark the hostile relation — 'enmity' (دشمنی) directed 'against' (بر) the capable/powerful (توانا); بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause sets the situation: hostility toward a powerful person existed.
segments.s557-g32: به پی is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of / after'; نسپرد is the negative past of سپردن ('did not entrust / did not send in pursuit') — here idiomatically 'did not pursue'; ویژه is adverbial/qualifying 'particularly / characteristic'; دانا بود means 'it was the wise [ones]' or 'it was proper of the wise' (بود = was). The whole conveys that it was characteristic of the wise not to pursue such enmity.
segments.s557-g41: This is a pair of imperatives addressed to listeners: جویید (second-person plural imperative) governs نام with همه in front as an adverbial/topic meaning ‘by all’ or ‘before everyone’ (i.e. ‘seek renown among all’). نیکی کنید is a coordinated imperative clause (کنید = do, act) meaning ‘perform goodness’ or ‘do good deeds.’ The conjunction و simply links the two commands.
segments.s557-g42: دل is the direct object ‘heart’. نیک‌پی is a descriptive compound meaning ‘good‑hearted / virtuous’ modifying the following noun phrase مردمان (‘people’). مشکنید is a negative imperative (classical form with initial م-) meaning ‘do not break’; the clause therefore orders ‘do not break the hearts of virtuous people.’ Word order places the object (دل نیک‌پی مردمان) before the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s557-g43: مرا is the dative/possessive topicalizer here (literally ‘to me’) indicating possession; the clause literally reads ‘to me treasure and dinars many are’ — i.e. ‘I have much treasure and money.’ هست is the copula/existential; بسیار modifies the quantity 'many'. Word order in classical Persian often puts the possessor (مرا) before the predicate.
segments.s557-g44: This is a nominal list completing the previous existential: ‘بزرگی و شاهی و نیروی دست’ — ‘greatness and kingship and the force/strength of the hand.’ The coordinating و links the nouns; نیروی + دست is a genitive-like compound meaning ‘the force/strength of the hand,’ here best understood as ‘military might / power.’
segments.s557-g45: خورید is a plural imperative (or hortative) meaning ‘eat’ used idiomatically as ‘enjoy / make use of.’ آنک (آن‌که) is a relative phrase ‘that which / what’ modifying دارید (‘you have’), so آنک دارید = ‘what you have.’ The second clause آن را که نیست = ‘that which (someone) does not have’ (introducing the person who lacks it in the next clause).
segments.s557-g46: بداند is a third-person present/subjunctive used here in a hortative/optative sense ‘let him know’ or ‘he should know.’ که introduces the content clause. با + گنج ما = ‘with our treasure’; او is the third-person pronoun ‘he’ or ‘that person’; یکیست (one‑is) states identity or equality: ‘he is one (with us) / he is equal.’ The whole clause: ‘let him know that with our treasure he is one.’
segments.s557-g47: سر بدره is a poetic collocation (literally ‘head/full‑moon’) describing a bright or prominent head/forehead; ما is the possessive/topic ‘our.’ گشادست is a predicative adjective ‘is wide/open,’ and باز here means ‘open’ (not the temporal ‘again’). Together the phrase states a proud or open countenance/position: ‘our head/brow is open/wide.’
segments.s557-g48: نباید is an impersonal modal ‘one must not / it ought not to be’; followed by the masdar/infinitive نشستن (‘to sit’). کس is the indefinite ‘anyone/someone’; اندر = ‘in/within’; نیاز = ‘need, want, supplication.’ The construction means ‘no one should sit in need,’ i.e. ‘no one ought to remain begging or in want.’
segments.s557-g17: سراسر is an adverb placed before the verb meaning 'entirely/altogether'. ببندید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative (classical/polite) of بستن here meaning 'shut' or 'stop'; it commands the addressee to cease an action. دست از هوا is an idiomatic object phrase ('hand from desire') = 'refrain from/stay away from desire', with از marking the source.
segments.s557-g18: هوا را places 'desire' as the (topical/definite) object with the postpositive را. مدارید is a classical hortative/imperative form addressing the ruler and here means 'indulge/treat gently' (i.e. 'do not pamper'); فرمانروا is a vocative/noun of address placed after the verb: 'O ruler'. The verb form is archaic/polite rather than the colloquial imperative.
segments.s557-g19: کسی کو is an archaic relative construction meaning 'whoever' / 'one who' (کو functions as a classical relative, not the modern 'where'). بپرهیزد is a 3rd-person-subjunctive/indicative form meaning 'keeps away / avoids' and is followed by از introducing the thing avoided; بدکنش denotes the bad action/behaviour that is to be avoided.
segments.s557-g20: نیالاید is a negative/defective classical verbal form meaning 'does not befall / will not come to pass' (here about misfortune not occurring). اندر introduces the locational/figurative domain 'into/among', followed by بدی‌ها 'evils' and تنش 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش = 'his body'), so the clause says his body will not fall into evils.
segments.s557-g21: بدین سوی is an adverbial phrase ('in this way / toward this side') built from the prepositional prefix ب- + دین + سوی; همواره is an adverb meaning 'always'. خرم بود is the predicate (adjective + past copula), 'was joyful' — the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s557-g22: گه رفتن is a temporal nominal clause 'when the going/departure (came)'; آیدش is 'came to him' (آید + enclitic ش), functioning as the verb of the temporal clause. بی‌غم بود is the result clause 'he was without sorrow / carefree', with بود as the past copula. The structure is classical: temporal clause followed by a descriptive main clause.
segments.s557-g23: پناهی بود is a nominal predicate 'was a refuge/shelter'. گنج را uses را to topicalize 'the treasure/treasury' as the beneficiary/thing sheltered; the following پادشا (king) is the logical subject in an inverted/classical order — 'the king was a refuge for the treasure.'
segments.s557-g24: نوازنده is a purposive/agent noun here 'one who soothes/comforts' or 'benefactor' rather than 'musician'; مردم پارسا is a genitive/appositive phrase 'the pious people'. Together this apposition characterizes the king as a comforter/patron of the pious.
segments.s557-g9: This is a short optative clause: noun phrase + predicate + optative particle. رخ (face) is the head noun, followed by the modifier بدسگالان (plural; ‘ill‑fated/ill‑omened ones’) and the possessor تو (poetic 'thou'). The adjective زرد (pale/wan) follows the noun phrase as is normal in Persian, and باد is the optative particle (literally 'may it be'), marking a wish.
segments.s557-g10: وزان is a conjunctive/temporal phrase here ('and/from that'), linking this clause to the previous wish. رفته is a past participle meaning 'gone/ departed' describing جان (soul, life). تو is the possessor 'your' (poetic 'thou'), بی‌درد is an adjective phrase 'without pain', and باد again is the optative particle ('may it be'). The whole segment is another wish: 'and from that may your departed soul be painless.'
segments.s557-g11: چنین داد پاسخ is a verb phrase in past tense: چنین ('thus') + داد (past of دادن, 'gave') + پاسخ ('answer'), so 'thus he gave answer' or 'he replied thus.' که‌ای here begins the quoted speech; ای is the vocative particle 'O' attached to the following noun, so که‌ای مهتران functions as the address 'O chiefs' (the que/که introduces the quoted clause).
segments.s557-g12: This is a vocative list naming the addressees. سواران جنگی is a noun + adjective ('riders/horsemen' qualified as 'warlike' or 'of war'), و is the conjunction 'and', and کنداوران is another plural noun added to the list (here 'those who dig/undertake digging' — i.e. trench‑diggers or excavators). The whole phrase is addressed to these groups.
segments.s557-g13: This segment lists the sources/people involved using the preposition ز (poetic 'from') and a coordinated structure: ز دهقان و ز مرد خسروپرست = 'from the peasant and from the Khosrow‑worshipping man.' Note that وز here is a poetic sequence of و + ز ('and from'). دهقان is the traditional landed/peasant class (dihqān), and خسروپرست describes men devoted to Khosrow (i.e. sycophants or royal worshippers).
segments.s557-g14: This is the main predication: به گیتی سوی (toward the world) بد modifies the action as 'evil/bad', and دست میازید is a verbal phrase meaning 'you stretch/lay your hand' or 'you lay hands on' (میازید is an imperfective/present form used here to describe repeated or ongoing action; it carries the sense 'to assail/afflict or to reach toward'). The literal order places the directional phrase before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s557-g15: بدانید is an imperative ('know' or 'be aware'), followed by the fused relative/ demonstrative کاین (که + این = 'that this' / 'that which is'), then چرخ ('the wheel'—here the cosmic wheel of fortune/fate) and the adjective ناپایدار ('unstable, transient') following the noun as usual in Persian.
segments.s557-g16: This is a correlative negative construction with نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...'). پرورده is a past participle used nominally here ('nurtured' / 'one who is nurtured' or 'that which is nurtured'), followed by داند — an archaic/polite present form functioning as the predicate verb (here best understood as 'is / has' or 'is considered'), and then نه پروردگار ('nor [is it] a nurturer/Lord'). The clause says the wheel has neither been nurtured nor does it nurture: it has no caretaker and is not a creator.
segments.s557-g1: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بهرام is the subject. بنشست is a past, 3rd-person-singular verb 'sat down'. The locative phrase بر تخت زر (preposition بر + noun تخت + postpositive qualifier زر) follows the verb, giving 'upon the golden throne.' Word order here is typical Classical Persian: conjunction – subject – verb – locative phrase.
segments.s557-g2: A verbless nominal clause describing Bahram's inner state: دل و مغز is a coordinated noun phrase 'heart and brain', followed by the adjective جوشان postpositively (meaning emotionally 'seething/boiling'), and the causal prepositional phrase ز مرگ پدر ('because of his father's death') where poetic ز = 'from/because of'. Possessive is implied by context (i.e. his heart/brain).
segments.s557-g3: همه is the quantifier 'all/everyone'. نامداران is a plural substantive meaning 'renowned ones' and ایرانیان identifies them as 'of the Iranians' (a genitive/attributive group). The whole segment is a noun phrase 'all the famed men of the Iranians.'
segments.s557-g4: برفتند is past, 3rd-person-plural 'they went/approached'. پیشش is an adverbial/oblique 'before him / in his presence' (the clitic -ش = 'him'). کمر بر میان is an idiomatic expression: کمر (belt/girdle) + بر میان ('to/at the waist/middle') implying 'they girded their loins' or 'made ready' — here functioning as a circumstantial participial phrase accompanying their approach.
segments.s557-g5: برو here precedes خواندند; in context it functions as an exclamatory/performative particle attached to the following utterance (literally 'go' but in classical Persian often an interjection signalling utterance). خواندند is past, 3rd-person-plural 'they recited/uttered'. آفرین خدای is the content of what they voiced: آفرین = 'bravo/acclaim', خدای = vocative 'O God'.
segments.s557-g6: که introduces the content clause ('that'). تا here has the temporal/conditional sense 'so long as' or 'until'. جای باشد is 'there be a place' with باشد a jussive/subjunctive form 'may be/there is'. تو مانی is an optative/subjunctive expression 'may you remain' (مانی is an archaic 2nd-person-singular form of ماندن 'to remain'); به جای means 'in the place' or 'in your station'. The clause therefore prays/wishes that he may remain in his place as long as there is a place.
segments.s557-g7: که again introduces a content clause 'that'. تاج کیی = 'the Kayanian crown' (کیی = Kayanian). تارکت is the verbal-noun/participle تارک with the 2nd-person enclitic -ت = 'your leaving/abandoning', so تارکت را is the marked object; سزاست is a predicative 'it is deserved/it is fitting'. Literarily: 'that the Kayanian crown, your leaving of it, is deserved' (i.e. fitting/deserved).
segments.s557-g8: پدر بر پدر is a repetitive/genitive expression 'father upon father' meaning 'by succession from father to father' (i.e. hereditary succession). پادشاهی 'kingship' is the subject/topic and تراست ('it is yours') is the predicative construction asserting that the kingship belongs to you. The line affirms hereditary right.

sentences.s557-s17: No good comes from a seeker of fame; avoid him and do not go about seeking quarrels.
sentences.s557-s18: Soldier and peasant and the idle — know, O king, thus: the three alike have no path.
sentences.s557-s19: The idle is buried in sleep; afterwards he becomes remorseful when he is awake.
sentences.s557-s20: From fair speech and foul deeds you will obtain neither praise nor the joy of paradise.
sentences.s557-s13: The king's body was a refuge for the faith, for the faith was like a hat upon his head.
sentences.s557-s14: Blessed is he who is more clear‑headed in anger; that same man is the less harmful on earth.
sentences.s557-s15: Sometimes, though the hand is scant, the heart is glad and generous; may the world never be without a wise man.
sentences.s557-s16: When enmity was directed against the powerful, the wise did not send anyone in pursuit (i.e., did not pursue it).
sentences.s557-s21: Seek fame and do good; do not break the hearts of good people.
sentences.s557-s22: I have much treasure and dinars, and greatness, kingship, and the might of the arm.
sentences.s557-s23: Consume/enjoy what you have, and let him who lacks it know that with our treasure he is one (i.e. equal).
sentences.s557-s24: Our brow (head) like the full moon is wide open again; no one should have to sit in need.
sentences.s557-s9: Completely withdraw your hand from desire; do not indulge desire, O sovereign.
sentences.s557-s10: Whoever keeps away from bad conduct will not have his body fall into evils.
sentences.s557-s11: In this state he was always joyful; when the time to go came, he was without sorrow.
sentences.s557-s12: The king was a refuge for the treasury, a comforter of the pious people.
sentences.s557-s5: May the faces of your ill‑fated ones grow pale, and may your soul, since it has gone, be free of pain.
sentences.s557-s6: He gave this reply: 'O chiefs, horsemen of war and trench‑diggers...'
sentences.s557-s7: You lay your hand toward evil in the world—from the peasant and from the Khosrow‑worshipping man.
sentences.s557-s8: Know that this unstable wheel is neither nurtured nor a nurturer.
sentences.s557-s1: When Bahram sat upon the golden throne, his heart and brain seething because of his father's death.
sentences.s557-s2: All the renowned men of the Iranians went before him, girding their loins.
sentences.s557-s3: They cried aloud, 'Bravo, O God! — that, so long as there is place, may you remain in your place.'
sentences.s557-s4: That the Kayanian crown is deserved by your having given it up; kingship, from father to father, is yours.
